perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Utah applicable to contracts executed and to be performed in the State of Utah with exception of its provisions regarding conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Utah with exception of its provisions regarding conflict of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'applicable to contracts executed and to be performed in the State of Utah' could create ambiguity. Utah law requires specific provisions and considerations for contracts actually executed and performed within Utah to protect consumers. Omitting this weakens consumer protections regarding jurisdiction and forum.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 13-11-4 outlines deceptive acts or practices in consumer transactions. By removing the clause specifying that Utah law applies to contracts executed and performed in Utah, the contract becomes less clear about whether the protections of the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act apply, potentially deceiving consumers into thinking they have fewer rights than they do.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-11-4 (2023) - Deceptive act or practice by supplier. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 11 - Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act Section 4 - Deceptive act or practice by supplier. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-11-4 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Effective 5/5/2021 13-11-4. Deceptive act or practice by supplier. (1) A deceptive act or practice by a supplier in connection with a consumer transaction violates this chapter whether it occurs before, during, or after the transaction. (2) Without limiting the scope of Subsection (1), a supplier commits a deceptive act or practice if the supplier knowingly or intentionally: (a) indicates that the subject of a consumer transaction has sponsorship, approval, performance characteristics, accessories, uses, or benefits, if it has not; (b) indicates that the subject of a consumer transaction is of a particular standard, quality, grade, style, or model, if it is not; (c) indicates that the subject of a consumer transaction is new, or unused, if it is not, or has been used to an extent that is materially different from the fact; (d) indicates that the subject of a consumer transaction is available to the consumer for a reason that does not exist, including any of the following reasons falsely used in an advertisement: (i) "going out of business"; (ii) "bankruptcy sale"; (iii) "lost our lease"; (iv) "building coming down"; (v) "forced out of business"; (vi) "final days"; (vii) "liquidation sale"; (viii) "fire sale"; (ix) "quitting business"; or (x) an expression similar to any of the expressions in Subsections (2)(d)(i) through (ix); (e) indicates that the subj
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that Utah law applies to contracts executed and performed in Utah. The changed text removes this specification, creating ambiguity as to whether Utah law applies to contracts executed and performed outside of Utah, which weakens consumer protections. The Law Explanation states that Utah Code Ann. § 13-11-4 outlines deceptive acts or practices in consumer transactions. By removing the clause specifying that Utah law applies to contracts executed and performed in Utah, the contract becomes less clear about whether the protections of the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act apply, potentially deceiving consumers. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions Utah Code § 13-11-4 (2023) which pertains to deceptive acts or practices by a supplier, reinforcing that the original clause was intended to protect consumers under Utah law, which is now weakened with the change. This falls under Omission Legal Contradiction, as it omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it potentially non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: The parties shall maintain complete confidentiality regarding each other's business and/or their affiliates and shall only disclose information pertaining to the Business Sources as permitted by Monnit, unless agreed and granted an expressed written permission of Monnit.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties shall maintain confidentiality regarding each other's business and/or their affiliates and shall only disclose information pertaining to the Business Sources.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'as permitted by Monnit, unless agreed and granted an expressed written permission of Monnit' removes an explicit permission structure for disclosure which could conflict with Utah's Uniform Trade Secrets Act regarding the necessary precautions for trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2 defines misappropriation and requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By removing the clause relating to Monnit's permission for disclosure, the contract weakens the safeguards for trade secret protection. If confidentiality is breached, it may become harder to prove that reasonable efforts were made to protect the trade secret under Utah law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2023) - Definitions. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes a clause that ensures disclosures are permitted by Monnit, which is crucial for maintaining the secrecy of trade secrets, as mandated by Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2. The original text allowed disclosure only with Monnit's permission, actively protecting confidential information. The changed text eliminates this safeguard. This omission contradicts the requirement to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Section 13-24-2(2)(b) defines 'misappropriation' to include disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without express or implied consent.' By removing the need for Monnit's permission, the contract weakens the trade secret protection, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of circumvention by any of the undersigned Parties, whether direct and/or indirect, the circumvented Party shall be entitled to a legal monetary compensation equal to ten times the maximum service fees it would have realized from such a transaction, plus any and all expenses, including any and all legal fees incurred in lieu of the recovery of such compensation.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of circumvention by any of the undersigned Parties, the circumvented Party shall be entitled to a legal monetary compensation plus expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause defining the compensation amount as 'ten times the maximum service fees it would have realized from such a transaction, plus any and all expenses, including any and all legal fees incurred in lieu of the recovery of such compensation' and only requiring 'a legal monetary compensation plus expenses' introduces ambiguity. Utah law requires clarity in contracts, particularly regarding damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah contract law - Requirement of definite and certain terms
perturbation.law_citation: Wagner v. Anderson, 2006 UT App 340, ¶ 16, 143 P.3d 285, 290
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Utah contract law (Wagner v. Anderson), contracts must have definite and certain terms to be enforceable. By removing the specific calculation for monetary compensation, the clause becomes vague, potentially rendering it unenforceable. This weakens the protection for the circumvented party, as the court may find the damages clause too uncertain.
perturbation.justification: The change omits the specific calculation for monetary compensation, which creates ambiguity and potentially renders the clause unenforceable. The original text specifies 'ten times the maximum service fees it would have realized from such a transaction, plus any and all expenses, including any and all legal fees incurred in lieu of the recovery of such compensation,' which is removed in the changed text. This omission falls under the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, which states that a legal contradiction occurs when a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement is valid for five (5) years from the date of signature, for any and all transactions between the Parties therein, with renewal to be agreed upon between the signatories.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement is valid indefinitely for any and all transactions between the Parties therein.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'five (5) years from the date of signature, with renewal to be agreed upon between the signatories' and making the agreement 'valid indefinitely' removes a key element that would cause the contract to expire, which could create enforceability issues under Utah contract law regarding reasonable duration and potential restraint of trade. Perpetual contracts are disfavored.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah law regarding contracts of indefinite duration
perturbation.law_citation: Resource Management Co. v. Weston Ranch and Livestock Co., Inc., 706 P.2d 1028, 1037 (Utah 1985)
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah courts (Resource Management Co. v. Weston Ranch) are hesitant to enforce contracts of indefinite duration, especially if they lack a clear termination mechanism. Making the agreement 'valid indefinitely' could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, as it perpetually binds the parties. This may violate the public policy against agreements that unduly restrict business activity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Wheeler by and Through Wheeler v. Mann :: 1985 :: Utah Supreme Court Decisions :: Utah Case Law :: Utah Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Wheeler by and Through Wheeler v. Mann Annotate this Case 763 P.2d 758 (1988) David Mark Newton WHEELER, a minor child, By and Through his Guardian ad Litem, Mark Wayne WHEELER, Plaintiff and Appellee, v. Stanley C. MANN, Defendant and Appellant. No. 19730. Supreme Court of Utah. June 30, 1988. Rehearing Denied November 4, 1988. Brent V. Manning, William D. Holyoak, Salt Lake City, for appellee. Stanley C. Mann, Salt Lake City, pro se. STEWART, Justice: This is an action by David Wheeler, the minor beneficiary of a testamentary trust, and his guardian father, Mark Wheeler, to recover the trust corpus and damages from *759 the trustee, Stanley C. Mann, for his breach of loyalty to the trust by self-dealing. The trustee appeals a summary judgment awarding the beneficiary $235,137.88, the amount of the trust fund plus interest, and attorney fees in the amount of $8,000. We affirm. Joan Wheeler, David's mother, established a testamentary trust naming David as the beneficiary and defendant Stanley Mann, her uncle, as the trustee. Joan and Mark Wheeler were divorced, and Joan was given custody of David Wheeler. Joan Wheeler died in December, 1978. Her death spawned several acrimonious lawsuits between Mann, Mark Wheeler, and others. Much of the dispute centered around the custody, control, and guardianship of David. After Joan Wheeler's death, defendant, as trustee, received trust assets of more than $167,000 in insurance proceeds. Over the next few years, defendant "invested," through loans and stock purchases, the entire trust corpus in two corporations, Western Marketing Resources, Inc., and Quest Publishing, Inc. Defendant is the president and managing officer of both entities. He is also the sole stockholder of Quest Publishing, Inc. Western Marketing Resources, Inc., is owned predominantly by him, his wife, and his sons; they are also its employees. The two business entities were ext
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a five-year term with renewal options. The changed text removes this fixed term, making the agreement valid 'indefinitely.' The Law Explanation states that Utah courts are hesitant to enforce contracts of indefinite duration, especially without a clear termination mechanism, as they may be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address perpetual contracts, the Law Explanation citing Resource Management Co. v. Weston Ranch supports the argument that indefinite contracts can be problematic under Utah law. By removing the defined term, the modified contract creates a potentially unenforceable agreement due to its indefinite nature, creating a legal contradiction.
